other_observers: Mark Peterson; Steve Mlodinow found the bird two days earlier.
specific_location: Barr Lake State Park, Barr Lake
description: This was a large tern as compared to the Forster's Tern that was nearby and the Cal Gulls it was roosting near. The large bill was orange without darkening towards the tip and it stood out in the early afternoon harsh light. The other most noticeable feature was the spiky black head feathers that extended in a narrow, but broadening line from behind the eye to the nape where they formed an unrully looking muff that contrasted strongly with the white head, cheeks, and hindneck. The rest of the face was white and the wing feathers were light gray. The legs were blackish. The folded primaries were a darker shade of gray than the light gray back. I saw it briefly in flight and could see what I thought were darker edges on the leading and trailing edge of the primaries.Behaviors: Other than a brief flight to change locations on the island beach where it loafed, the tern mostly stood at attention by Cal Gulls and Canada Geese.Call: no calls heardPlumage: early molt - maybe winter adult
